perturbation.original_text: (a) Basic Fee. In consideration for the Service provided hereunder, for --------- each package shipped utilizing the Service (other than a package which is shipped first class by the U.S. Postal -9- Service), the Company shall be entitled to receive the amount of [***]* (the --- "Basic Fee"). MBE agrees to use its commercially reasonable efforts to execute --------- agreements, or to facilitate the execution of agreements among the appropriate parties, so that such Basic Fee is paid to the Company directly from the carrier which ships such package no later than fifteen (15) days following the end of the month in which such package was shipped.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) Basic Fee. In consideration for the Service provided hereunder, for --------- each package shipped utilizing the Service (other than a package which is shipped first class by the U.S. Postal -9- Service), the Company shall be entitled to receive the amount of [***]* (the --- "Basic Fee"). MBE agrees to use its commercially reasonable efforts to execute --------- agreements, or to facilitate the execution of agreements among the appropriate parties, so that such Basic Fee is paid to the Company directly from the carrier which ships such package no later than forty-five (45) days following the end of the month in which such package was shipped.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline from 15 days to 45 days following the end of the month may create non-compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Prompt Payment Law for Construction Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7108.5 requires payments to subcontractors within seven days of the contractor receiving payment, unless otherwise agreed in writing. This change extends the payment period beyond what is generally considered acceptable and may violate this prompt payment law if the service is considered a construction-related activity or if MBE is considered a subcontractor.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 7. Disciplinary Proceedings [7090 - 7124.6] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) 7108.5. (a) A prime contractor or subcontractor shall pay to any subcontractor, not later than seven days after receipt of each progress payment, unless otherwise agreed to in writing, the respective amounts allowed the contractor on account of the work performed by the subcontractors, to the extent of each subcontractor’s interest therein. In the event that there is a good faith dispute over all or any portion of the amount due on a progress payment from the prime contractor or subcontractor to a subcontractor, the prime contractor or subcontractor may withhold no more than 150 percent of the disputed amount. (b) Any violation of this section shall constitute a cause for disciplinary action and shall subject the licensee to a penalty, payable to the subcontractor, of 2 percent of the amount due per month for every month that payment is not made. (c) In any action for the collection of funds wrongfully withheld, the prevailing party shall
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment deadline to subcontractors from 15 days to 45 days. This contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 7108.5(a), which states that a prime contractor or subcontractor shall pay any subcontractor no later than seven days after receipt of each progress payment, "unless otherwise agreed to in writing". The 'otherwise agreed to in writing' clause does not override the initial seven-day requirement unless explicitly agreed upon. The changed text extends the payment deadline significantly, creating a legal contradiction. The law snippet specifies a 7 day deadline, and the contract creates a 45 day one, unless agreed in writing.

perturbation.original_text: The Company shall pay such amounts, less the relevant Bounty Fee or eBay Fee then in effect, to MBE on the next business day or as soon as reasonably practicable (but in any event within one week) following the shipment date of each package, and MBE agrees to distribute such amounts to the MBE Centers.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall pay such amounts, less the relevant Bounty Fee or eBay Fee then in effect, to MBE at the Company's discretion, and MBE agrees to distribute such amounts to the MBE Centers when it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the specific payment timeframe (within one week) and replacing it with discretionary payment terms may violate wage payment laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code Section 204 mandates that wages earned during a calendar month shall be paid between the 1st and 15th day of the following month. By removing the specific timeframe and implementing discretionary payment, the modified text violates this requirement, as the payment could potentially be delayed beyond the legally mandated timeframe.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 204. (a) All wages, other than those mentioned in Section 201, 201.3, 202, 204.1, or 204.2, earned by any person in any employment are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays. Labor performed between the 1st and 15th days, inclusive, of any calendar month shall be paid for between the 16th and the 26th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and labor performed between the 16th and the last day, inclusive, of any calendar month, shall be paid for between the 1st and 10th day of the following month. However, salaries of executive, administrative, and professional employees of employers covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act, as set forth pursuant to Section 13(a)(1) of the Fair Labor Standards Act, as amended through March 1, 1969, in Part 541 of Title 29 of the Code of Federal Regulations, as that p
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces a specific payment timeframe with discretionary payment terms, potentially violating California Labor Code Section 204, which mandates wages earned between the 1st and 15th of the month be paid between the 16th and 26th of the same month, and wages earned between the 16th and last day of the month be paid between the 1st and 10th of the following month. The original text states payment should occur within one week of shipment, while the changed text allows the Company to pay 'at the Company's discretion,' which could delay payment beyond the legally mandated timeframe. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the changed text creates a time-sensitive obligation that violates state law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 clearly outlines the required payment schedule, which the changed text undermines.

perturbation.original_text: MBE shall, at any time during the term of this Agreement, be entitled to audit all such records upon ten (10) days written notice to the Company, in order to confirm the accuracy of such records and conformance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement; provided, however, that no more than one (1) such audit may be conducted in any -------- ------- ninety (90)-day period.
perturbation.changed_text: MBE shall, at any time during the term of this Agreement, be entitled to audit all such records upon sixty (60) days written notice to the Company, in order to confirm the accuracy of such records and conformance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement; provided, however, that no more than one (1) such audit may be conducted in any calendar year.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the audit notice period to 60 days and limiting audits to once per year may unreasonably restrict MBE's ability to verify compliance and accuracy.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing (California)
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statutory violation, significantly restricting audit rights can be argued as a violation of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, which California law imposes on all contracts. This covenant requires parties to act fairly and honestly in their contractual dealings. By severely limiting the frequency and increasing the notice period for audits, the modified text could be interpreted as hindering MBE's ability to ensure accurate payments and compliance, thus breaching the implied covenant.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [1101 - 1310] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1963, Ch. 819. ) CHAPTER 3. Territorial Applicability and General Rules [1301 - 1310] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 30. ) 1304. Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. (Added by renumbering Section 1203 by Stats. 2006, Ch. 254, Sec. 19. Effective January 1, 2007.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an inconsistency that creates a legal contradiction. The original contract allowed for audits every 90 days with 10 days' notice, while the changed contract allows for audits only once per year with 60 days' notice. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1, from the California Commercial Code (COM 1304), states: 'Every contract or duty within this code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.' By severely restricting the audit frequency and increasing the notice period, the modified text could hinder MBE's ability to ensure compliance, potentially breaching this implied covenant.
